name: Migrate from CommonJS to ES Modules
description: Convert a Node.js project from require/module.exports to import/export syntax with proper configuration.

steps.title: Set module type in package.json
steps.description: Add the type field to package.json to tell Node.js to treat .js files as ES modules by default.
steps.tip: With type: module, all .js files use ESM. Use .cjs extension for files that must remain CommonJS.

steps.title: Convert require statements to import
steps.description: Replace require() calls with import statements and module.exports with export.

steps.title: Fix __dirname and __filename references
steps.description: Replace __dirname and __filename with ESM equivalents since these globals do not exist in ES modules.
steps.warning: __dirname and __filename are not available in ESM. Every file that uses them needs the import.meta.url workaround.

steps.title: Add file extensions to relative imports
steps.description: ESM requires explicit file extensions on relative imports. Update all local import paths.
steps.command:
# ESM requires extensions on relative imports:
# Before: import { handler } from './routes/api'
# After:  import { handler } from './routes/api.js'

# Find all relative imports missing extensions:
grep -rn "from '\./" src/ --include='*.js' | grep -v "\.js'" | grep -v "\.json'"
steps.warning: This is the most tedious part. Every relative import must include .js even if the source file is .ts (TypeScript resolves .js to .ts).

steps.title: Handle JSON imports
steps.description: JSON imports require an import assertion in ESM mode.
steps.tip: The createRequire workaround works on all Node.js versions and is useful for packages that only support CommonJS.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# CommonJS to ESM Migration Helper

echo "==> Analyzing project for CJS patterns..."

echo ""
echo "==> require() calls found:"
grep -rn "require(" src/ --include='*.js' --include='*.ts' 2>/dev/null | wc -l

echo ""
echo "==> module.exports found:"
grep -rn "module.exports" src/ --include='*.js' --include='*.ts' 2>/dev/null | wc -l

echo ""
echo "==> __dirname/__filename usage:"
grep -rn "__dirname|__filename" src/ --include='*.js' --include='*.ts' 2>/dev/null | wc -l

echo ""
echo "==> Relative imports missing .js extension:"
grep -rn "from '\./\|from "\.\./" src/ --include='*.js' --include='*.ts' 2>/dev/null | grep -v "\.js['"]" | grep -v "\.json['"]" | grep -v "\.mjs['"]" | wc -l

echo ""
echo "==> JSON requires:"
grep -rn "require.*\.json" src/ --include='*.js' --include='*.ts' 2>/dev/null | wc -l

echo ""
echo "Migration steps:"
echo "  1. Run: npm pkg set type=module"
echo "  2. Rename files that must stay CJS to .cjs"
echo "  3. Convert require() -> import"
echo "  4. Convert module.exports -> export"
echo "  5. Replace __dirname with import.meta.url"
echo "  6. Add .js extensions to relative imports"
echo "  7. Update JSON imports with assertions"
echo "  8. Run: node --experimental-vm-modules app.js (if using vm)"

faq.question: Can I mix ESM and CommonJS in the same project?
faq.answer: Yes. With type: module in package.json, use .cjs for CommonJS files. Without it, use .mjs for ESM files. ESM can import CJS modules, but CJS cannot require() ESM modules (use dynamic import() instead).

faq.question: Do I have to migrate all at once?
faq.answer: No. You can migrate incrementally by using .mjs extension for converted files while keeping the project as CJS (no type: module). Or set type: module and use .cjs for files not yet converted.
